children.text: This method executes a Python script directly using
python script.py

to specify a different virtual environment path. Use

Python applications automatically have debugging support enabled.

members.name: AddPythonExecutable
members.signature: public static IResourceBuilder<PythonAppResource> PythonAppResourceBuilderExtensions.AddPythonExecutable(this IDistributedApplicationBuilder builder, string name, string appDirectory, string executableName)

summary.text: Adds a Python executable to the application model.

children.text: This method runs an executable from the virtual environment's bin directory. By default, the virtual environment folder is expected to be named
.venv
and located in the app directory. Use
to specify a different virtual environment path. Use
WithArgs
to pass arguments to the executable.
Unlike scripts and modules, Python executables do not have debugging support enabled by default. Use
to explicitly enable debugging support if the executable is a Python-based tool that can be debugged.

appDirectory.text: The path to the directory containing the python application.

executableName.text: The name of the executable in the virtual environment (e.g., "pytest", "uvicorn", "flask").

description.text: Add a pytest executable to the application model:

summary.text: Adds a Python module to the application model.

children.text: This method runs a Python module using
python -m <module>
. By default, the virtual environment folder is expected to be named
.venv
and located in the app directory. Use
to specify a different virtual environment path. Use
WithArgs
to pass arguments to the module.
Python modules automatically have debugging support enabled.

appDirectory.text: The path to the directory containing the python application.

moduleName.text: The name of the Python module to run (e.g., "flask", "uvicorn").

description.text: Add a Flask module to the application model:

members.name: AddUvicornApp
members.signature: public static IResourceBuilder<UvicornAppResource> PythonAppResourceBuilderExtensions.AddUvicornApp(this IDistributedApplicationBuilder builder, string name, string appDirectory, string app)

summary.text: Adds a Uvicorn-based Python application to the distributed application builder with HTTP endpoint configuration.

children.text: This method configures the application to use Uvicorn as the ASGI server and exposes an HTTP endpoint. When publishing, it sets the entry point to use the Uvicorn executable with appropriate arguments for host and port.
By default, the virtual environment folder is expected to be named
.venv
and located in the app directory. Use
to specify a different virtual environment path.
In non-publish mode, the
--reload
flag is automatically added to enable hot reload during development.

returns.text: A resource builder for further configuration of the Uvicorn Python application resource.

app.text: The ASGI app import path which informs Uvicorn which module and variable to load as your web application. For example, "main:app" means "main.py" file and variable named "app".

appDirectory.text: The directory containing the Python application files.

builder.text: The distributed application builder to which the Uvicorn application resource will be added.

name.text: The unique name of the Uvicorn application resource.

description.text: Add a FastAPI application using Uvicorn:

summary.text: Enables debugging support for the Python application.

children.text: This method adds the
to the resource, which enables debugging support. The debugging configuration is automatically set up based on the entrypoint type (Script, Module, or Executable).
The debug configuration includes the Python interpreter path from the virtual environment, the program or module to debug, and appropriate launch settings.

members.name: WithEntrypoint
members.signature: public static IResourceBuilder<T> PythonAppResourceBuilderExtensions.WithEntrypoint<T>(this IResourceBuilder<T> builder, EntrypointType entrypointType, string entrypoint)

summary.text: Configures the entrypoint for the Python application.

children.text: This method allows you to change the entrypoint configuration of a Python application after it has been created. The command and arguments will be updated based on the specified entrypoint type:

description.text: Script
: Runs as
python <scriptPath>
Module
: Runs as
python -m <moduleName>
Executable
: Runs the executable directly from the virtual environment

children.text: Important:
This method resets all command-line arguments. If you need to add arguments after changing the entrypoint, call
WithArgs
after this method.

entrypoint.text: The entrypoint value (script path, module name, or executable name).

entrypointType.text: The type of entrypoint (Script, Module, or Executable).

description.text: Change a Python app from running a script to running a module:

members.name: WithPip
members.signature: public static IResourceBuilder<T> PythonAppResourceBuilderExtensions.WithPip<T>(this IResourceBuilder<T> builder, bool install = true, string[]? installArgs = null)

summary.text: Configures the Python resource to use pip as the package manager and optionally installs packages before the application starts.

children.text: This method creates a child resource that runs
pip install
in the working directory of the Python application. The Python application will wait for this resource to complete successfully before starting.

Pip will automatically detect and use either pyproject.toml or requirements.txt based on which file exists in the application directory. If pyproject.toml exists, pip will use it. Otherwise, if requirements.txt exists, pip will use that. Calling this method will replace any previously configured package manager (such as uv).

install.text: When true (default), automatically installs packages before the application starts. When false, only sets the package manager annotation without creating an installer resource.

installArgs.text: The command-line arguments passed to pip install command.

T.text: The type of the Python application resource, must derive from

description.text: Thrown when
is null.
Add a Python app with automatic pip package installation:

members.name: WithUv
members.signature: public static IResourceBuilder<T> PythonAppResourceBuilderExtensions.WithUv<T>(this IResourceBuilder<T> builder, bool install = true, string[]? args = null)

summary.text: Adds a UV environment setup task to ensure the virtual environment exists before running the Python application.

children.text: This method creates a child resource that runs
uv sync
in the working directory of the Python application. The Python application will wait for this resource to complete successfully before starting.

UV (https://github.com/astral-sh/uv) is a modern Python package manager written in Rust that can manage virtual environments and dependencies with significantly faster performance than traditional tools. The
uv sync
command ensures that the virtual environment exists, Python is installed if needed, and all dependencies specified in pyproject.toml are installed and synchronized.
Calling this method will replace any previously configured package manager (such as pip).

args.text: Optional custom arguments to pass to the uv command. If not provided, defaults to ["sync"].

install.text: When true (default), automatically runs uv sync before the application starts. When false, only sets the package manager annotation without creating an installer resource.

T.text: The type of the Python application resource, must derive from

description.text: Thrown when
is null.
Add a Python app with automatic UV environment setup:
Add a Python app with custom UV arguments:

members.name: WithVirtualEnvironment
members.signature: public static IResourceBuilder<T> PythonAppResourceBuilderExtensions.WithVirtualEnvironment<T>(this IResourceBuilder<T> builder, string virtualEnvironmentPath, bool createIfNotExists = true)

summary.text: Configures a custom virtual environment path for the Python application.

children.text: This method updates the Python executable path to use the specified virtual environment.
By default (
true
), if the virtual environment doesn't exist, it will be automatically created before running the application (when using pip package manager, not uv). Set
to
false
to disable this behavior and require the venv to already exist.
Virtual environments allow Python applications to have isolated dependencies separate from the system Python installation. This is the recommended approach for Python applications.

When you explicitly specify a virtual environment path using this method, the path is used verbatim. The automatic multi-location lookup (checking both app and AppHost directories) only applies when using the default ".venv" path during initial app creation via AddPythonScript, AddPythonModule, or AddPythonExecutable.

createIfNotExists.text: Whether to automatically create the virtual environment if it doesn't exist. Defaults to
to disable automatic venv creation (the venv must already exist).

virtualEnvironmentPath.text: The path to the virtual environment. Can be absolute or relative to the app directory. When relative, it is resolved from the working directory of the Python application. Common values include ".venv", "venv", or "myenv".

description.text: Configure a Python app to use a custom virtual environment:

summary.text: Specifies the type of entrypoint for a Python application.

enumMembers.name: Executable
enumMembers.description: A standalone executable from the virtual environment (e.g., "uvicorn", "flask", "pytest").

enumMembers.name: Script
enumMembers.description: A Python script file to execute directly (e.g., "main.py", "app.py").

enumMembers.name: Module
enumMembers.description: A Python module to run via python -m <module> (e.g., "flask", "uvicorn").

summary.text: Represents a Python application resource in the distributed application model.

children.text: This resource allows Python applications (scripts, web servers, APIs, background services) to run as part of a distributed application. The resource manages the Python executable, working directory, and lifecycle of the Python application.

Python applications can expose HTTP endpoints, communicate with other services, and participate in service discovery like other Aspire resources. They support automatic OpenTelemetry instrumentation for observability when configured with the appropriate Python packages.
This resource supports various Python execution environments including:

appDirectory.text: The working directory for the Python application. Python scripts and modules will be resolved relative to this directory. This is typically the root directory of your Python project containing your main script and any local modules.

executablePath.text: The path to the Python executable. This can be:
The executable is typically located in a virtual environment's bin (Linux/macOS) or Scripts (Windows) directory.

name.text: The name of the resource in the application model.

description.text: Add a Python web application using Flask or FastAPI:

summary.text: Represents a Python application resource in the distributed application model.

children.text: This resource allows Python applications (scripts, web servers, APIs, background services) to run as part of a distributed application. The resource manages the Python executable, working directory, and lifecycle of the Python application.

Python applications can expose HTTP endpoints, communicate with other services, and participate in service discovery like other Aspire resources. They support automatic OpenTelemetry instrumentation for observability when configured with the appropriate Python packages.
This resource supports various Python execution environments including:

appDirectory.text: The working directory for the Python application. Python scripts and modules will be resolved relative to this directory. This is typically the root directory of your Python project containing your main script and any local modules.

executablePath.text: The path to the Python executable. This can be:
The executable is typically located in a virtual environment's bin (Linux/macOS) or Scripts (Windows) directory.

name.text: The name of the resource in the application model.

description.text: Add a Python web application using Flask or FastAPI:

summary.text: Represents a Uvicorn-based Python application resource that can be managed and executed within a Python environment.

children.text: This resource is specifically designed for Python web applications that use Uvicorn as their ASGI server, which is commonly used with frameworks like FastAPI, Starlette, and other ASGI applications.
The resource automatically configures HTTP endpoints and sets up appropriate Uvicorn-specific command-line arguments for host binding and port configuration.

appDirectory.text: The working directory for the Uvicorn application. This is typically the root directory of your Python project containing your ASGI application module.

executablePath.text: The path to the Python executable. This is typically the Python executable within a virtual environment.

name.text: The unique name used to identify the Uvicorn application resource.

children.text: This resource is specifically designed for Python web applications that use Uvicorn as their ASGI server, which is commonly used with frameworks like FastAPI, Starlette, and other ASGI applications.
The resource automatically configures HTTP endpoints and sets up appropriate Uvicorn-specific command-line arguments for host binding and port configuration.

appDirectory.text: The working directory for the Uvicorn application. This is typically the root directory of your Python project containing your ASGI application module.

executablePath.text: The path to the Python executable. This is typically the Python executable within a virtual environment.

name.text: The unique name used to identify the Uvicorn application resource.
